posts.name: JM San Roman
posts.cooked:
My issue here is: shouldn’t a wine magazine be capable of providing a rating, a valuation of the quality of a wine independently from the personal taste of the reviewer?

I may prefer Priorat or Bordeaux to Rioja, but if I review a good wine from Rioja I will try to provide as objective a review as possible, beyond my personal preferences.

I have seen a massive change in ratings and appreciation of Bordeaux wines with the change in coverage that took place at TWA one or two years ago. Some (many) wines that were highly appreciated and highly rated were suddenly downgraded and replaced by the newly favored Chateaux.

And now it is Rhone. Yesterday night I had a bottle of Le Clos du Pic 2016, from Chateau Puech-Haut. An XXL wine which I very much liked in its style (certainly not for the faint of heart). So I went today to TWA to check the rating and I see that it got 94 points at the time and that this particular wine has received normally between 94 and 96 points from Joe Czerwinski. The 2018 vintage (which was a hot complicated vintage in the area) got 92-94.

But wait! Then I see no review for 2019 and a new reviewer for 2020 rating it 88 points and with a footnote saying that this Chateau’s wines “sometimes lack elegance and finesse”.

Is there anything like an objective valuation of the quality in a wine or must we be 100% subject to the preference and subjectivity (bias) of the wine critic?

I know I am implicitly referring to some critics who are very popular in this forum, but my question is an honest one. Will now wines in the US be re-rated because Joe is reviewing there? Will wine ratings in Portugal be turned around now Luis Gutiérrez has taken over? Which ratings were correct: the old ones or the new ones?

Mind you: Joe and Luis are not the ones I am criticising here; I love them!

It happens all the time and everywhere - also and especially here in this forum.
Praise for some wines from one side, damnation and accusation of “turning to the dark side” from the other.
It seems to depend not on any quality in bottle, but on a the subjective philosophy how wine should be or being made - or not be made:
from ripe fruit (and appropriate alcohol) - or with moderate alc, high acidity and a certain astringency etc

( I m exagerating here)

For me personally any rating and any report is worth as much as the taste of the taster - what’ s the value of a rating of a vegetarian about a steak? Or of the chef in a steak house about falafel with tofou?

posts.name: JM San Roman
posts.cooked: Good example, Paul. If you replace “vegetarian” for “Burgundian” and “steak” for “Bordeaux” you get perfectly what I mean.

posts.name: Mike Evans

Is there anything like an objective valuation of the quality in a wine or must we be 100% subject to the preference and subjectivity (bias) of the wine critic?

posts.name: D@vid K@ntr0witz

posts.name: SeanSydney
posts.cooked:
Yes and no. I don’t really want a critic who doesn’t have preferences or a perspective on wine (with the caveat that they have to be able to define and elucidate what they consider to be high-quality) but they should not go out of their way to fête something they don’t believe is deserving just because some appreciate the style.

We can all “appreciate” a competently-made wine and acknowledge that it will appeal to a particular group of people, but if the critic genuinely believes that a wine is hot, oaky, unbalanced, etc then they should say so and one can take away what they will from it.

I think it’s far less valuable to both a publication and their readers for a critic to abrogate their responsibility to, you know, critique the wines they review. Someone with a platform and a clear sense of what is or isn’t a quality wine shouldn’t feel the need to couch or hedge.

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked:
I agree with John. So do most aesthetic theorists of any note. Critics tend not to, probably because accepting that what one does is subjective tends to undercut the value of one’s claims. It is, at any rate the case, that there are no agreed upon criteria for evaluating artworks. Disputes over values thus quickly become disputes over criteria and then table pounding about how everybody else of note agrees with you.

The situation with wine is obviously worse. Not only are criteria not agreed on (what’s in the glass, accuracy of reflection of terroir, made according to this method or that one?), but, finally, one experiences it purely through taste and taste is ineluctably variable. If you don’t like brussel sprouts and I do, there is no method of judging between us.

For your ends, however, there is a solution. If you like Puech-Haut, then the wine is objectively good as far as you are concerned and you should buy it. Critics provide word of mouth, not objective conclusions. Find one whose word of mouth you like, if you must, and use him or her as a guide to decide what to taste.

posts.name: Chris_Rickey
posts.cooked: “Word of mouth”: I like that. That was clever.

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
JuanS:

My issue here is: shouldn’t a wine magazine be capable of providing a rating, a valuation of the quality of a wine independently from the personal taste of the reviewer

No. That’s the entire point. It’s the personal taste of a reviewer that makes you want to subscribe, or not.

All, or most reviewers should have their own likes, dislikes and peccadillos that you either agree with, or not.

If the WA or any reviewer does not work for you, don’t subscribe.

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
M.Kaplan:

Live by points, die by points. The whole idea of assigning some sort of alleged objective scoring system to the subjective matter of personal taste is foolish and a recipe for disappointment.

I do not agree. Scores from a reviewer are that critics opinion on how a particular wine measures against another. Where’s the problem?

Take me for example. A lot of folks buy wines I like, while another group of peeps purchases wines I loathe. It works as long as the writer is consistent and provides commentary (tasting notes) backing up their views.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Jeff_Leve:
M.Kaplan:

Live by points, die by points. The whole idea of assigning some sort of alleged objective scoring system to the subjective matter of personal taste is foolish and a recipe for disappointment.

I do not agree. Scores from a reviewer are that critics opinion on how a particular wine measures against another. Where’s the problem?

Take me for example. A lot of folks buy wines I like, while another group of peeps purchases wines I loathe. It works as long as the writer is consistent and provides commentary (tasting notes) backing up their views.

I agree, Jeff. I find the note and the score both very helpful, especially when evaluated together. Ultimately, points are not going away, so we can either embrace it, reject it or wallow in the misery of the constant debating about it. I find the hundred point scale very intuitive, really not much different than the grading scale most of us grew up with in school, and frankly what most of us were exposed to when we got into wine appreciation in the era of Robert Parker and the Wine Spectator. But as Jeff notes, if you are going to place any value on the rating of a critic, you need to know that critic and appreciate his or her palate.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
1. Points and objectivity are two separate issues. One doesn’t have to believe it’s possible to have “objective” ratings to find points valuable if you know the palate of the person assigning them.

  2. I, too, respect critics who can say, “This isn’t my style, but for those who like this style, it’s a good wine.” But I think it’s awfully hard to assign an exact point score to a wine you feel that way about.

  3. I also respect critics like John Gilman who have strong palate leanings and make no bones about it. (He’s not inclined to say, “This 15.5% wine tastes like raisin juice. It’s not my preferred style, but some will love this.”) I don’t always agree with John’s take, but his reviews are valuable to me.

posts.name: JM San Roman
posts.cooked:
That is great, Jeff, as long as there is sufficient stability in the critic’s coverage as TheWineCellarInsider has enjoyed over the years. But what would happen if, say, the critics change every two or three years and the ratings go on a rollercoaster from 85 to 95 and back to 85? How informative would the ratings be then?

As @John_Morris said, I find is perfectly acceptable to state that “this is not my preferred style, but it is a good wine and some will love it”. I understand there will always be a great degree of subjectivity but moving from “this is a great wine” to “this is a poor wine” is simply not helpful. Inconsistent.

posts.name: Cris Whetstone
posts.cooked:
It depends on how you are using said critics notes and articles.

If one wants a buying guide that consistently maps the same wines vintage to vintage in order to guide your purchases, then fluctuating scores are likely to drive you nuts. The scores are the important information and not having some predictability will frustrate your goals.

If one wants to learn about wines, including differences in vintages then the scores are less likely to be an issue. What the critic writes now and over time are what are paramount. That’s where you get the important information.

posts.name: Philip N. Jones
posts.cooked:
Hooray for scores! I find them very useful, particularly once I find a critic whose palate agrees with mine. If you don’t like scores, don’t read them. Or read them, then ignore them.
Each to their own!!

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
JuanS:

guess you can do like Luis Gutierrez does: he says “this is not my preferred type of wine but in its style it’s very good”… And he gives a reasonable rating.

Which is utter bullshit! It is hard enough to evaluate a wine fairly given your own likes and dislikes. To decide to extrapolate to someone else’s palate is nearly impossible. I want a critic to tell me what he/she thinks, not tell me what he decides someone else might think.

Complaining about different palates means you need to do some work. Figure out how Antonio, Jeb, James, Joe, Lisa etc align with your own palate.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
I think the biggest issue with TWA is not so much the changes of personnel (which are inevitable, and of course they all have their own tastes) as the curious decision to publish all scores as “RP”. Quite apart from the fact that none of them are RP clones and are entitled to their own opinions, plus the incredible disrespect it entails, it is misleading for consumers and IMO, is blatant misinformation which is used, or misused, by countless commercial ventures who promote wines as having “95 points by RP”. Obviously, no reviewer had or has RP’s taste and no current reviewer has the same taste as any predecessor. Seems fairly logical.

Hopefully, as new editor-in-chief, WK will have a say about changing this policy.

posts.name: D Borczon
posts.cooked:
I would be thrilled if an influential critic downgraded a wine I liked to the high 80’s! Just think of the discount prices in the future. I suppose if it influenced the winery to radically change style that would be unhelpful for you. Can we please get WK off the Burgundy beat and get some critic to devalue Lafon, PYCM, Guyon, Leroy, etc,maybe prices could be like the good old days!!!

To some extent this appears to have happened in both Australia and the southern Rhone. When Parker jumped all over these producers prices shot up. Since “different tastes” have influenced much of the mainstream wine press, the speculators and hard core collectors have gone elsewhere to chase the dragon. In some respects this is the battle that vintage port is fighting. Has port gotten worse over time? It probably has actually improved in the style it expresses, certainly not gone backwards. You can pick up V Port in great back years for less than the release price (adjusted for inflation). Some houses are shifting to lower alcohol table wines to cope with the changing tastes.

Who knows with the current trends, 12.5% pinot may be viewed as “heavy and alcoholic” by the next generation weaned on 5% seltzer water…

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
Dennis_Borczon:

In some respects this is the battle that vintage port is fighting.

…

Who knows with the current trends

Yes Port seems to be facing an even tougher environment than before, and it’s always been a wine that despite ageing wonderfully, never seems to see rapid price increases for cellared bottles. Sherry has worked hard to reinvent itself, and port in theory an easier sell.

As for the trends, Australia has in recent decades been cyclic, from celebrating ripeness (at times falling over into over-ripeness), to seeking lighter alc. and greater elegance. The big shows often were the battleground for hearts and minds, with the appointment of the head judge giving a hint as to which direction the wind was blowing.

Whilst I could imagine a time when alcohol was side-lined as much as tobacco is now, I think that will be a long way off. Low and no alcohol wines have generally been disappointing, though some traditional styles show it can be done without the dubious input of ‘science’ (e.g. Lambrusco, Moscato, traditional German riesling, though those latter two now run up against the odd modern backlash against sweetness, which I’d assumed was driven by crappy sugared piesporter and liebfraumilch, but seems to remain today). Certainly climate change is forcing a re-think about location and viticulture, and there’s still something of a backlash against the Parker-era big and alcoholic wines. Hence I’m not entirely convinced we’ll see alc% continue to drive down to the point that even the old days of 12-13% become the norm.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Your post raises a lot of very interesting questions that go to the heart of wine criticism, and some important transitions happening at TWA—though I can’t help forming the impression that you’ve already made up your mind about the answers (and if you really do consider contemporary Burgundy to be the wine world’s answer to vegetarian cuisine, our capacity for agreement may be limited). I’d prefer to answer many of those questions in our own time, but know that at TWA we are certainly thinking about them very keenly. So I’ll confine myself to some more general remarks.

As Chanel said, “fashions change, but style endures”, and the reality is that the wine world has (by her definition) plenty of fashion along with plenty of style. We don’t, for the most part, listen to the same popular music as our parents, or seek out the same cuisine; we don’t decorate our homes the same way, or take the same vacations. Wine isn’t any different, and I make no apology for championing what I think is a contemporary vision of wine. It would be very unhealthy, to my mind, to try to freeze tastes in the year 2000 and judge wines by the criteria of another era. Many great producers have evolved immeasurably since then, whether it’s Peter Sisseck at Pingus, the team at Harlan Estate, Troplong-Mondot in Saint-Émilion, or Christophe Roumier in Chambolle. That’s natural, and to me, laudable.

Similarly—and this has been discussed before—the notion that there is no legitimate role for taste in wine criticism is quite simply nonsense. Do architectural critics admire a building because the roof doesn’t leak? Do film critics praise a film because the cameras are in focus? Is a musical performance great as long as the instruments are in tune? Or is a béarnaise profound simply because the egg yolk hasn’t curdled? No other domain of aesthetic criticism has fallen for this canard. So the idea that wines are entitled to praise simply for being “well made” (and at that, by your definition, not mine) doesn’t hold much water.

I think if you’re trying to reduce this evolution to some simplistic polarity between “big wines” and “weedy wines” you’re not going to enjoy much success. My own cellar contains plenty of old Quintarelli, Henri Bonneau Grange des Pères and Graeme MacDonald’s Cabernets, and Emmanuel Reynaud’s wines are in the top-five by volume. Last night, I opened a 2007 Prüm WS Spaëtlese, a 2008 Pignan and a 1969 Ponsot Clos de la Roche—so my tastes aren’t so susceptible to being pigeonholed. Some of the highly-rated Bordeaux in TWA today are too powerful for some of the Berserkers reading this. What I hope is that the wines I rate highly express a strong identity, delivered with harmony, purity, integration and structural seamlessness/finesse. The flip side is that I have indeed downgraded wines that to me seem to be dominated by their élevage; which display overtly overripe characteristics; and/or which are marred by elevated levels of brettanomyces-derived off-aromas. My arguments are given, I think, quite clearly in my reviews, as I think it’s important to give my reasoning—precisely so people can identify which step of my argumentation they agree or disagree with. I’m always happy to discuss them

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked: Just remember that the term ‘objective wine review’ is, and will always be, an oxymoron . . .

posts.name: JM San Roman
posts.cooked:
Thanks for your reply, William. I appreciate you taking up the gauntlet.

I still believe that there can be some degree of objectivity on what a good wine is (not just well made) even if its élevage is more marked that what one’s personal taste errs towards as long as it is made “with harmony, purity, integration and structural seamlessness/finesse” as you say. I believe it is compatible.

Because one can always wonder who’s taste has been frozen in the wrong era: Joe’s, Yohann’s, Lisa’s or yours? Call me a romantic but I would love to believe there is some degree of objectivity amongst the tastes of the four of you. Or Jeff’s or Jebb’s or Neal’s, to agree on what a good wine is. Not just well made. Even if each of you prefers a different style.

I have absolutely nothing against Burgundy nor against vegetarian cuisine, God forbid! I love a good French Pinot! But I do have a daughter who went through a vegan phase and at the time I would not dare to ask her for the best meat restaurants in town. Nor would I have asked her to become the steakhouse specialist at Time Out.

Having said all that, I have to admit that by reading your wine reviews I have progressively come fairly close to understanding which wines I will/will not like beyond the specific rating (“number”) that you give.

I just wish you would sometimes be able to use a more prudent, less disdainful tone when writing the reviews of these objectively good wines that you dislike because you (in your own right) disagree with their style. I guess it is something one achieves in due course with age, like a good wine does.

posts.name: D@vid K@ntr0witz
posts.cooked: While I don’t find William’s reviews to be disdainful in the least, the notion that the problem in contemporary wine criticism is that critics are too negative towards wines not made in their preferred style is wild to me. If anything, the problem is the opposite - that critics, for a variety of reasons, are too positive. I would welcome a little more disdain, frankly, but I’m under no illusions we’re going to get it.

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

As Chanel said, “fashions change, but style endures”,

Says a great fashion designer hoping to be something more. Style endures only in the sense in which Buffon meant it when he says le style c’est l’homme. It is the manifestation of individual character that goes into the work artists produce. It can’t be generalized, so it can’t really ever be an evaluative criterion, except to the extent that one appreciates the manifestion of this or that individual. So, yes, there are winemakers who have a distinctive signature so to speak. But whether one likes how the signature manifests itself is another issue.

posts.name: larry schaffer

And please note that these tasting notes are never blind - and therefore ‘bias’ is bound to creep in, whether anyone wants it to or not. I’m not saying that’s a bad thing - but if you try a wine from a producer that ‘normally produces high score wines’ and your score is really low, there may be some issues in posting it. And that goes over the same winery being reviewed by different reviewers from the same publication.

Cheers

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
Greg_K:

I believe the point William was, in part, trying to make is that there is no such thing as an “objectively good wine”.

My experience with a long running blind tasting group shows that typically 6 of 8 wines have first and last place votes. Sometimes a wine one person absolutely adores will be repulsive to another. We’ve had a few tastings where all of the wines were excellent to everyone’s opinions, so differentiation was shades of grey. Some wines will be universally loved by a group with great diversity of preference. Some will be universally disliked. There’s just a full spectrum that most people don’t/didn’t see with the dominance of a few critics (and virtually no printed scores for sub-90 pt wines). A lot of social groups have domineering alpha-types, where a lot of people are afraid to express dissent, so there’s an illusion of universal like for a wine. A lot of people are intimidated, whether they lose confidence in their own palate or just don’t see the point in speaking up.

posts.name: P eter L owet
posts.cooked:
JuanS:

I don’t! As I already said I am happy that I have been able to buy reasonably priced Pontet-Canet.

Actually, recent Pontet Canet is a great counter-argument to your point and is perfect validation of WIlliam’s point!

  • P-C has had a strong run of highly rated wines, particularly since they went organic/biodynamic.
    Their winemaking recently changed further with amphorae, etc.
  • Critics differ on the benefits of these latest changes.
  • William has laid out his view clearly and put his points where his mouth is.

As a result, if you think either that he’s “objectively” wrong or that his tastes don’t align with yours, you have a great buying opportunity because the market has spoken with lower prices for recent releases/futures. And you’ll also save money in subscription fees.

Meanwhile, I’m curious: Have prices for back vintages (well-scored by prior RP/WA critics), e.g. 2010, been impacted by lower scores of recent vintages (beyond overall softening of the Bordeaux market)? That would be a bit weird, but not WK’s fault.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
JuanS:

Thanks for your reply, William. I appreciate you taking up the gauntlet.

I still believe that there can be some degree of objectivity on what a good wine is (not just well made) even if its élevage is more marked that what one’s personal taste errs towards as long as it is made “with harmony, purity, integration and structural seamlessness/finesse” as you say. I believe it is compatible.

Because one can always wonder who’s taste has been frozen in the wrong era: Joe’s, Yohann’s, Lisa’s or yours? Call me a romantic but I would love to believe there is some degree of objectivity amongst the tastes of the four of you. Or Jeff’s or Jebb’s or Neal’s, to agree on what a good wine is. Not just well made. Even if each of you prefers a different style.

I have absolutely nothing against Burgundy nor against vegetarian cuisine, God forbid! I love a good French Pinot! But I do have a daughter who went through a vegan phase and at the time I would not dare to ask her for the best meat restaurants in town. Nor would I have asked her to become the steakhouse specialist at Time Out.

Having said all that, I have to admit that by reading your wine reviews I have progressively come fairly close to understanding which wines I will/will not like beyond the specific rating (“number”) that you give.

I just wish you would sometimes be able to use a more prudent, less disdainful tone when writing the reviews of these objectively good wines that you dislike because you (in your own right) disagree with their style. I guess it is something one achieves in due course with age, like a good wine does.

I really cannot think of anything more useless as an “objective” wine review. My mind kind of first goes to a wine that objectively gets a score of 100 points that nobody actually enjoys.

But also, I have no interest in an objectively great wine. I want to buy wines that I will enjoy, not wines that you or anyone else can enjoy. If you really want to buy wines that you will like, YOU have to find reviewers whose palate aligns with yours.

Too many people think confuse objectively great wines that all critics should like with wines they like. [Maybe the 88 is THE objectively correct for your wine, not the 95.] Don’t make that mistake. You like what you like, as you should. But don’t confuse what you like with what is objectively great. I have a wonderful tasting group and have tasted with others, including Jeff Leve, who know a lot about wine. Once in a while, we all agree about what is the best wine or wines at a tasting. But not that often. Who is right? Well, me of course. Only I have THE objective palate. LOL

posts.name: J@son Tr@ughber
posts.cooked:
M_Benjamin:

What do you mean by “dominated by their elevage” please?

I took this to mean “the winemaking obliterates the terroir”.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Dominated by élevage = when a wine is dominated on the nose or palate by exogenous aromas imparted during its maturation. Most obviously, that would be wines dominated by vanillin/lactones/char aromas from new oak, with coarse wood tannins on the finish; but it could also be the taste of old wood (chestnut-like aromas in red, sourdough starter-like aromas in white), or the VA you’re liable to get from excessively porous amphorae, or whatever. The goal of élevage is to clarify, stabilize, refine, and resolve a wine’s redox equilibrium before bottling, but a less successful élevage can detract, sometimes fatally, from a wine’s purity of expression.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Ah, objectivity. As they said in the Princess Bride, “you keep using that word, I do not think it means what you think it means”. An “objective” quality is a quality of the object (in this case, the wine) in itself that exists independently of an observer. Objective information about a wine would include things like its alcohol content, pH, blend, release price, so on. There is plenty of such information in a tasting note, but the core work of any appreciation/criticism of a wine is inherently subjective, in that it perceived by a subject (the taster) and not a property of the wine in and of itself. For example, that the release price is 150 USD is objective; that the wine is expensive is subjective. Some subjective perceptions can be tethered in objective fact: if the wine is aged entirely in heavily toasted new barrels (objective), and the critic observes that it tastes oaky (subjective), this at least seems a priori plausible. And some subjective views are so widely shared as to become a consensus; but this does not make them objective. Nor does subjectivity imply incoherence or caprice: a good critic aspires to be very consistent in their point of view and reasoning. In this particular case, you are criticizing me for having said that certain wines that are objectively late-picked, above-average in alcohol and aged in appreciable percentages of new oak taste borderline overripe, alcoholic and oaky, because a critical consensus held that they were not, and accusing me of lacking objectivity. So you see we have gotten quite a long way from what the words actually mean. As you say, it is possible that with age I will come to share your tastes, but I think readers will be entitled to wonder whether that represents growing wisdom or a declining palate.

posts.name: Yule Kim
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

Dominated by élevage = when a wine is dominated on the nose or palate by endogenous aromas imparted during its maturation. Most obviously, that would be wines dominated by vanillin/lactones/char aromas from new oak,

Do you mean “exogenous aromas” if the aromas come from an external factor like new oak?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: I do! I’ll blame autocorrect…

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
John_Morris:
Craig_G:

Maybe not for those, but for books about the New York Subway, we have that.

Did I hear my name mentioned?

I’m still trying to figure out how I ordered two of them, and neither was signed!

Hey, did I tell you, my son loved King of Capital? So cool that he read it.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

As you say, it is possible that with age I will come to share your tastes, but I think readers will be entitled to wonder whether that represents growing wisdom or a declining palate.

Is this up for vote?
If so, sharing his taste, put one down for declining palate.

posts.name: Chris_Rickey
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

Dominated by élevage = when a wine is dominated on the nose or palate by exogenous aromas imparted during its maturation. Most obviously, that would be wines dominated by vanillin/lactones/char aromas from new oak, with coarse wood tannins on the finish; but it could also be the taste of old wood (chestnut-like aromas in red, sourdough starter-like aromas in white), or the VA you’re liable to get from excessively porous amphorae, or whatever. The goal of élevage is to clarify, stabilize, refine, and resolve a wine’s redox equilibrium before bottling, but a less successful élevage can detract, sometimes fatally, from a wine’s purity of expression.

Part of the problem of the “dominated by” paradigm is that (i) “dominated by” vs. “enhanced by” are matters of judgment ultimately shaped by what is perceived by the taster as pleasant and (ii) the perception of such “endogenous” (leaving aside for the moment what counts as endogenous and what counts as exogenous in winemaking) aromas/flavors can change over time.

As for the first, I realize that you have attempted to address this point in your subsequent post about “objective” vs. “subjective” and that in the end such matters of judgment are unavoidable, but I think Juan’s point is that a critic should strive to have the purity of wine criticism not be dominated by such purely individual factors. His initial example of the 2020 Clos du Pic was actually an interesting example of the phenomenon. We have a new reviewer, a tasting note that was frankly rather anodyne (the same words could have gone with a 95 point review or the 88 points YC gave it), and given that it was a new reviewer, we the readers lacked the context to know if the 88 points was because of a bad vintage or, as the producer note and the framing of the short article suggests, that the wine was “dominated by” excessive oak. Unfortunately for this comparison, Jeb hasn’t reviewed the wine yet, but let’s pretend that based on prior reviews he would have given it a 95. 88 vs. 95 is a pretty big gap for the same wine, particularly if that gap is driven almost exclusively by what the reviewers perceive as “excessive” vs. “tolerable” levels of oak.

For the second point, let me use an example to illustrate. A few years back, I drank a bottle of Realm Bard. It was the quintessential blueberry-vanilla milkshake, in this case, so dominated by vanilla it was too much even for me, someone who loves vanilla flavors. So, to use your terms, a wine dominated by its oak signature. I tried another bottle of the same vintage a few years later. The vanilla had been subsumed into the wine to the point it was just the barest whisper and the toasting was no longer apparent. So it was a wine no longer dominated by the oak signature. At what point is the critic supposed to measure when the elevage has interfered with the wine expression? At the time of tasting is the obvious answer, but if the result is that you would score the wine an 87 on the initial tasting and a 95 on the second, you have introduced a measure of variability into your scoring that hasn’t been present in other critics. It’s not like Jeb or Lisa wouldn’t have picked up the oak signatures; they just ignored the present dominance to project what it would be like in its peak drinking window. That projection is, of course, a matter of judgment that may not pan out. Put another way, I find all young Bordeaux (and all young Napa cabs and merlots) to be dominated by their elevage and have assumed that you, like your predecessors, are projecting the judgment about “too much” or “too little” of anything into that peak drinking window.

posts.name: Chris_Rickey
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

The goal of élevage is to clarify, stabilize, refine, and resolve a wine’s redox equilibrium before bottling, but a less successful élevage can detract, sometimes fatally, from a wine’s purity of expression.

I’m curious how that paradigm would handle the example of the two normal top-end wines at Pierre Usseglio, the Mon Aieul and Deux Freres. The latter is brought up in 100% small oak barrels, while the former is aged predominately in concrete tanks, with some portion aged in large, old oak foudres. They are deliberately different expressions of the same underlying wine. It would be odd to say that the Deux Freres expression is “marred” by the oak, since that is intrinsic to the expression. Many people on this board, however, would say that the Deux Freres is marred by the oak because the choice to express a Chateauneuf du Pape in small oak barrels is intrinsically a flawed expression. Robert Parker gave both wines very high scores, and thus was obviously comfortable concluding that both were very successful expressions of CNdP. The paradigm you outlined could match either of those outcomes, which is why I’m curious.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: I think over the last two decades, my profession as a whole has given the benefit of the doubt a bit too frequently to some extremely overtly oaky wines that have, in many cases, remained that way today. As RP used to write on the cover of TWA, I would rather underrate than overrate a wine, and I tend to be quite critical on this point—or should I say, I have high expectations. I don’t agree that all young Bordeaux are, or need to be, dominated by their élevage, and indeed one of the impressive things about estates such as Lafleur, Canon, Palmer and others is how much progress they have made in better integrating new oak in their wines. This, by the way, has very little to do with the percentage of new wood itself, and much more to do with the choice of barrels and how they are made, how the wines are barreled down, and a host of other details.

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked: Yes and no. Since Descartes, most philosophers believe that all knowledge we have comes through subjective apprehension. In 18th century empirical philosophy, the shift was all we know of the world comes through our sensations, which, in Bishop Berkeley, became a form of complete idealism. The task in the 19th century became one of finding procedures to sort out those subjective apprehensions that one could say corresponded to certain facts about objects. Those sensations would correspond to objective knowledge, but they would still be subjectively apprehended. At the time and since, there have been claims that various evaluative systems, for art, morals, etc. could be formulated that would so correspond. Your position, which comes from logical positivism, continues to have a long run. But it is not the only one. I do agree with you that wine evaluation is subjectively variable, but I don’t think Juan is wrong because he doesn’t define the terms the way you and some 20th century philosophers do.

posts.name: Charlie Carnes
posts.cooked: The last thing in the world I want from a “pro” taster is objectivity. I want his opinion on how it tastes to him, how he reacts to the wine, and tasting circumstances. His consistent subjectivity is how I can align my palate to his. If he tasted and wrote any other way, that would be about as useful as a piece of paper on a fishing trip.

posts.name: Yule Kim
posts.cooked:
The description in the note is more valuable than the actual score. Specifically, descriptions related to texture, acidity, oakiness, etc… I also really appreciate William including technical information as well (PH levels, percentage of new oak) because that does help inform my own buying decisions.

Scores are really only useful within a tranche of wines being reviewed, so you can know relatively which wines showed better within the tasting. No one should treat scores as objective measures that have relevance beyond a particular tasting.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
Yule_Kim:

The description in the note is more valuable than the actual score. Specifically, descriptions related to texture, acidity, oakiness, etc… I also really appreciate William including technical information as well (PH levels, percentage of new oak) because that does help inform my own buying decisions.

Scores are really only useful within a tranche of wines being reviewed, so you can know relatively which wines showed better within the tasting. No one should treat scores as objective measures that have relevance beyond a particular tasting.

Agreed - insight is more use to me than the personal preference of the taster. It is in this direction I would encourage Juan to push - asking reviewers to write with a view to informing the reader, rather than persuading them.
